claim: Pi has a sub-1000 token system prompt compared to Claude Code's ~14,000 token system prompt, resulting in larger usable context windows
actionable: Choose Pi over Claude Code if maximizing context window size is a priority for your coding agent workflows

claim: Pi's extension system (TypeScript files in .pi/agent/extensions) allows customization of UI, tools, and workflows, and agents can read Pi's own documentation to build extensions for themselves
actionable: Use Pi's extension system to encode deterministic multi-step workflows as extensions, avoiding repetitive back-and-forth prompting with the agent

claim: Pi ships with only 4 core tools (read, bash, edit, write) because most coding tasks can be accomplished via bash, and this minimalism simplifies agent design
actionable: When designing agents, consider whether a minimal tool set focusing on bash execution is sufficient before adding specialized tools

claim: Pi does not support bringing a paid Anthropic subscription; Anthropic-based access requires API keys, making it more expensive than using a subscription for API calls
actionable: If using Anthropic models with Pi, plan for API-based costs rather than subscription benefits; consider OpenRouter or other multi-model providers as alternatives

claim: Workflow extensions enable agents to run deterministic multi-step pipelines (spec → implement → review → fix → test) with separate context windows for each stage
actionable: Build workflow extensions to encode software development best practices (code review, testing) as deterministic agent steps rather than relying on repeated manual prompts

claim: Skills in Pi are on-demand capabilities stored in external repos (rather than scattered across system folders), making them easier to manage and swap between projects
actionable: Maintain a centralized external skills repository and reference it in Pi config rather than scattering skills across multiple directories

claim: Extensions are used for code-running tasks (intercepting tool calls, UI customization, tool registration), while skills are used for instruction injection for specific task types (e.g., code review, refactoring)
actionable: Use extensions for behavioral customization and skills for task-specific prompt templates; avoid conflating the two

claim: Pi runs with full access by default (no permission prompts), following the philosophy that permission gates are often clicked through mindlessly
actionable: If security is critical, build custom permission gate extensions in Pi rather than relying on built-in permission dialogs
